storm.summary_layperson: Dangerous thunderstorms are sweeping directly over the cove right now, with a Tornado Watch in effect until 9 PM and a second round of organized severe weather expected from the west through the early evening — there is no coastal buffer to weaken storms here tonight.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Long Island Sound water (71°F) is nearly as warm as the air (73°F), so the marine layer that sometimes slows incoming storms is absent — and with 45-knot wind shear through the atmosphere, any organized cell approaching from the west can arrive at full strength.
storm.detail_html:
A severe convective outbreak is underway across the region, driven by a warm front lifting north through the area this afternoon and evening. Radar at 4:19 PM showed storm cells as close as 0.2 miles from Greenwich Cove at reflectivities reaching 62 dBZ — the intensity range associated with large hail and damaging winds. Rain rates at the cove dock station exceeded 6 inches per hour at last reading. A Tornado Watch is active until 9:00 PM, a Severe Thunderstorm Warning runs through 5:00 PM, and a Flash Flood Warning is in effect until 7:15 PM.

The current storm cells are tracking north-northwest at roughly 7 knots and pulling away from the cove. However, the NWS Upton forecast office explicitly warns of organized, upstream severe thunderstorm activity capable of reaching the western Sound between 5 and 9 PM — exactly the 17:00–22:00 window. If the warm front pushes far enough north to allow a brief lull in activity, conditions become more favorable for that upstream convection to hold together on approach. Damaging wind gusts are the primary severe threat; the environment also supports isolated brief tornadoes.

There is no local factor softening this risk. Long Island Sound water is only 2°F cooler than the air (71°F vs. 73°F), so no meaningful stable marine layer exists over the water today. On cold-water days — when the Sound runs 10°F or more below air temperature — this layer can weaken or deflect incoming storms before they reach the coast. That mechanism is not available tonight. Atmospheric wind shear is 45 knots or more through the lower atmosphere, and storm energy (CAPE) is near 2,200 joules per kilogram. Any organized cell approaching from the west or southwest has the ingredients to maintain full intensity all the way to the cove.

What would lower the risk: if the warm front clears well north before 5 PM and the local instability is consumed by the current storm activity, leaving the organized upstream convection without enough fuel to sustain itself into the area. The NWS considers this possible but not certain. Until the 5–9 PM window closes without a new round of storms, the risk remains high.

wind.regime_label: Light, post-storm gradient — no sea breeze, direction will wander
wind.tactics_blurb: Post-storm winds tonight will be light and directionless — no favored side, so stay central, keep options open, and treat storm risk as the primary variable; conditions could reset completely if a new cell arrives.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Tonight is not a sea-breeze day. The air–water temperature difference is just 2.2°F (air 73°F, water 71°F) — far below the threshold needed to drive a meaningful thermal sea breeze, and the sea-breeze direction gate is not met. The post-storm environment will instead produce light, highly variable winds through the 17:00–22:00 window as the atmosphere reorganizes behind the convective activity. This is a light gradient-wind situation where the synoptic pattern provides almost no sustained push in any direction, and post-storm mesoscale chaos fills in with direction scatter.

The HRRR 19Z forecast shows winds cycling through NNW, ENE, SE, SSE, and SSW across the five primary hours of the window — a roughly 180° sweep in direction with speeds barely reaching 8 knots at peak. This level of directional scatter reflects both the HRRR's difficulty resolving light-wind patterns and the storm-influenced mesoscale environment. Actual conditions at each hour will depend heavily on which cells are active nearby and how the warm-frontal boundary shifts. Treat the forecast directions as rough guidance, not precise calls.

Sunset is at approximately 8:04 PM EDT, falling just after the 20:00 hour. On a normal sea-breeze day, the thermal collapses around this time — tonight that transition is irrelevant since there is no meaningful sea breeze in play. What sunset does mean: temperatures ease slightly, any residual gradient weakens further, and the post-sunset hours (20:00–22:00) are likely to be the lightest — unless a new storm cell resets conditions with a gust front. The 22:00 row comes from HRRR extended range output (lower confidence) and should be treated as a rough guide only.

Tactically: the cove's geography provides no leverage when wind direction is shifting 180° across the window. There is no pressure band or consistent lift to chase toward either side of the course. Stay central, cover early shifts quickly without committing deep, and sail to the mark rather than to a side. Be prepared for sudden changes in speed and direction if storm activity approaches — a gust front can arrive with 20–30 knots from an unexpected direction with little notice.

The dominant uncertainty for this window is the storm outlook, not the wind regime. If organized severe weather moves through between 17:00 and 21:00 as the NWS discussion warns, race-tactical wind considerations become secondary to safety. Treat the storm risk as the primary variable and adjust tactics around whatever conditions actually materialize.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 last wind obs 75 min old — readings predate the storm and do not reflect current conditions
KGON (Groton) and KWST (Westerly) anemometers offline — eastern Sound wind data unavailable
RYC rain rate 6.19 in/hr at fetch time — active storm over the cove; post-storm wind evolution is highly uncertain
HRRR wrfsfc does not include gust field — hourly gusts estimated at approximately 1.8× mean speed
